"""
scene_config.py
================
Central configuration for the black hole simulation.

All physical distances are expressed in units of the Schwarzschild
radius (u_rs = 1.0). Keeping everything in these "natural units" makes
the geodesic integration in the shader numerically well behaved and
makes the visual proportions (photon sphere, ISCO, disk extent)
independent of any real-world unit system.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class SceneConfig:
    # ---- Window / rendering ----
    window_size: tuple = (1600, 900)
    window_title: str = "Real-Time Black Hole Simulation"
    vsync: bool = True
    samples: int = 0          # MSAA handled manually via AA supersampling instead
    aa_samples: int = 1       # 1 = single sample/pixel, 2 = 2x2 supersampling (4 taps)

    # ---- Adaptive performance (keeps frame rate >= target_fps on any GPU) ----
    target_fps: float = 55.0           # comfortably clears the "50 fps or higher" bar
    min_render_scale: float = 0.55     # lowest internal render resolution multiplier
    min_march_steps: int = 130         # lowest allowed geodesic step ceiling

    # ---- Black hole physics (Schwarzschild, natural units, rs = 1.0) ----
    schwarzschild_radius: float = 1.0
    photon_sphere_radius: float = 1.5      # 1.5 * rs, informational / used for glow shaping
    isco_radius: float = 3.0               # innermost stable circular orbit (non-spinning)
    capture_radius_factor: float = 0.98    # ray considered captured just inside rs

    # ---- Accretion disk ----
    disk_inner_radius: float = 3.0         # == ISCO
    disk_outer_radius: float = 11.0
    disk_rotation_speed: float = 1.15      # overall multiplier on Keplerian angular velocity
    disk_turbulence_scale: float = 2.6
    disk_brightness: float = 2.9

    # ---- Camera ----
    cam_distance: float = 9.5
    cam_min_distance: float = 2.6
    cam_max_distance: float = 45.0         # MUST stay comfortably below escape_radius (see above)
    cam_azimuth: float = 0.35
    cam_elevation: float = 0.28
    cam_fov_deg: float = 45.0
    cam_min_fov_deg: float = 15.0
    cam_max_fov_deg: float = 100.0
    cam_smoothing: float = 12.0            # higher = snappier, lower = floatier

    # ---- Starfield ----
    star_density: float = 1.0
    star_twinkle_speed: float = 1.0

    # ---- Post-processing ----
    exposure: float = 1.25
    bloom_threshold: float = 1.2
    bloom_strength: float = 0.8
    bloom_blur_passes: int = 4             # number of horizontal+vertical blur pairs

    # ---- Ray marching quality ----
    max_march_steps: int = 190             # ceiling; AdaptivePerformance can pull this down further
    escape_radius: float = 65.0            # multiples of rs; MUST stay > cam_max_distance (below) with
                                            # margin, or the camera could zoom out past the "infinity"
                                            # sphere and the black hole would never get ray-marched at all

title: Event Horizon Explorer (Inspired from Interstellar)
author: A S Aditya
tagline: Navigate around a black hole and witness gravity reshape the universe through gravitational lensing.
description: This simulation is an interactive visualization of a black hole surrounded by a glowing accretion disk. As the camera moves around the black hole, the immense gravitational field bends the paths of light, producing realistic gravitational lensing effects predicted by Einstein's theory of General Relativity. The accretion disk appears warped, duplicated, and wrapped around the event horizon, allowing viewers to observe how extreme gravity distorts our perception of space. Rather than presenting a static rendering, the simulation lets users experience the black hole from multiple viewpoints, revealing different lensing patterns and visual phenomena as the perspective changes. Combining real-time rendering with physically inspired light bending, the project offers an engaging way to explore one of the universe's most fascinating objects while making complex astrophysical concepts intuitive and visually compelling.

title: Dying Blackhole and a title eaten
author: Dharshini Subramani
tagline: Evaporation of Blackhole due to Hawking radiation and sphagettification of the title
description: This is an interactive simulation of a blackhole dying /evaporating due to Hawking radiation. This can serve as a fun educational material for kids and contains a cool item where the title is being eaten up by the blackhole when the slider is being used. The physics modules used in this simulation is gravitational lensing, kerr black hole physics, accretion disk and hawking radiation.

title: Project Gargantua
author: Nischay
tagline: An interactive GPU-accelerated simulation of how spacetime bends around a Schwarzschild black hole.
description:
Project Gargantua is an interactive visualization of a Schwarzschild black hole built to make general relativity both beautiful and intuitive. Instead of treating a black hole as a simple sphere, the simulator models how extreme gravity curves spacetime, causing light rays to bend, accretion disks to distort, and the familiar black hole shadow to emerge naturally.

The simulation uses GPU-accelerated Taichi kernels together with physically motivated models for gravitational lensing, relativistic Doppler beaming, gravitational redshift, and a thin accretion disk based on the Novikov–Thorne model. A cinematic observer mode allows users to approach the event horizon and experience relativistic effects such as gravitational time dilation while exploring the system from multiple viewpoints.

My goal was not simply to create a visually impressive black hole, but to build an educational tool that demonstrates how Einstein's theory of general relativity produces many of the phenomena seen in modern astrophysics. Wherever practical, the visualization follows established physics, with approximations clearly documented where full numerical relativity would be beyond the scope of a real-time simulation.

title: Into the Abyss
author: Charita
tagline: A black hole trying to be a black hole
description: A real-time interactive visualization that demonstrates how a black hole influences its surroundings using physically inspired astrophysical principles. The simulation models an event horizon, an orbiting accretion disk, and gravitational lensing, showing how intense gravity bends light and distorts the appearance of distant stars. Users can interact with the scene by adjusting the camera and simulation parameters to explore different perspectives. The project combines physics, computer graphics, and GPU-accelerated computation in Python using Taichi to create an educational experience that is both scientifically grounded and visually engaging, making complex concepts accessible through immersive visualization.

title: Einstein Sandbox
author: Abishag
tagline: An interactive space playground where you can shoot lasers, launch planets, and watch them instantly warp and loop around heavy black holes in real time.
description:
Einstein Sandbox is a fun, click-and-drag space game where you get to break the rules of normal physics and warp reality. It works by simulating how giant objects actually bend space, letting you shoot laser beams, launch custom planets, and click on stars to implode them into heavy black holes. 
You can click various tools like photons that allow you to generate particles of light and see how they bend, planets and dust allow you to generate them in the sim, give. yourself more debris to work with!! Pull lets you use your mass cursor to have its own gravitational pull, lets see if you can fight against the effects of a black hole. You can also use a slider to adgust the G force.

title: Silent Superradiance
author: Maanya Chhabra
tagline: Black Hole Dynamics in an Axion Field
description:
Silent Superradiance is an interactive visualization inspired by one of the most intriguing predictions in black hole physics: the extraction of rotational energy from a rapidly spinning Kerr Black Hole through superradiance. Rather than acting solely as a cosmic sink this simulation explores the idea of the black hole as an energy source, transferring part of its rotational energy to a surrounding axion cloud while gradually losing its spin. This reversal of the familiar "black hole only consume" narrative forms the foundation of the visualization. 
This visualization combines an orbiting accretion disk, a gravitationally lensed photon ring, an evolving standing wave axion cloud, and outward propagating gravitational wave inspired ripples into a real time scene. A live scientific interface allows users to vary the black holes spin and axion coupling strength while observing how these quantities influence the system.

title: TON 618
author: Bisman Singh
tagline: An Interactive Simulation of TON 618 Black Hole
description:
This is an interactive, physics inspired real time simulation of one of the universe's most massive known supermassive black holes TON 618 . Built in Python using the Taichi GPU programming framework, it delivers stunning volumetric rendering and realistic relativistic effects at ultra-high performance.

How it Works
The simulation utilizes advanced GPU ray marching through a Schwarzschild metric, calculating real-time gravitational lensing (light bending) around the event horizon. It simulates the accretion disk's volumetric density and incorporates relativistic Doppler beaming where the disk dynamically shifts in brightness and color (bluish white when approaching, dim violet when receding) depending on the orbital velocity. It also models polar plasma jets streaming along the rotation axis.
 
Why it's Cool
The simulation is highly interactive, featuring custom sidebar controls to adjust the black hole's mass, disk density, and jet velocity in real-time. It runs incredibly fast on GPU/Metal using pre computed noise textures for Fractional Brownian Motion (FBM) shader calculations.

title: Seeing the Invisible
author: Har Ridam Singh
tagline: Visualizing the Physics Beyond Human Sight.
description:
"Seeing the Invisible" is an interactive real-time visualization of blackhole physics that transforms complex astrophysical phenomena into an immersive experience. Built with GPU acceleration, the simulation recreates key features of a blackhole system, including a glowing accretion disk, gravitational lensing, photon ring, Hawking radiation, and pulsar emissions. Users can interact with the simulation by spawning test particles, exploring different visualization modes, and observing how matter and light behave in regions of extreme gravity. 
NOTE:-
IT WILL TAKE TIME TO OPEN BUT IT WILL OPEN

title: Dance of Titans: A Cinematic Binary Black Hole Merger Simulation
author: Aiden Vihaan
tagline: Witness two black holes spiral, merge, and ring down through a cinematic real-time WebGL simulation inspired by modern astrophysics.
description:
Dance of the Titans is an interactive real-time visualization of a binary black hole merger built with Three.js and WebGL.

The simulation recreates the complete lifecycle of a binary system—from inspiral and orbital decay to merger and ringdown—through physically inspired orbital dynamics, cinematic rendering, GPU-accelerated particle systems, post-processing effects, and immersive telemetry.

Key Features

• Real-time binary black hole simulation
• Physically inspired orbital dynamics
• Dynamic accretion disks and particle systems
• Gravitational-wave inspired telemetry visualization
• Cinematic camera mode with automatic tracking
• HDR bloom, depth of field, and post-processing
• Interactive observatory controls
• Educational astrophysics visualization
• Responsive WebGL rendering

Technologies Used

• Three.js
• WebGL
• JavaScript (ES6)
• GLSL Shaders
• HTML5
• CSS3

Dance of the Titans was created as a scientific visualization project to demonstrate one of the universe's most energetic astrophysical events while combining modern computer graphics, interactive storytelling, and educational exploration.

title: Star to Singularity
author: Bodhi Bipin
tagline: interactive simulation of stellar evolution, quasar formation, and the exponential growth of black holes.
description: **Star to Singularity** is an interactive simulation that lets you explore the dramatic birth and growth of a black hole. The journey begins with a massive dying supergiant star moments before it explodes in a spectacular supernova, scattering a vast cloud of glowing gas into space. As the explosion fades, gravity pulls the remaining core together, forming a young black hole surrounded by an accretion disk. You can interact with the simulation by feeding particles into the black hole. Each particle is color-coded by temperature—red represents hot matter, while blue represents cooler matter—allowing you to observe how different material behaves as it spirals inward. As the black hole consumes more matter, it grows larger and more energetic. However, adding too many particles causes the system to become unstable, launching powerful, corkscrew-shaped jets of plasma perpendicular to the accretion disk, creating a spectacular quasar powered by the black hole's immense energy.

title: Helios
author: Nishika Shukla
tagline: Interactive Solar Magnetic Reconnection Simulator
description:
**Helios** is an interactive educational simulation that visualizes **solar magnetic reconnection**—a fundamental plasma physics process in which magnetic field lines break, reconnect, and release stored magnetic energy. Designed to make this complex astrophysical phenomenon more accessible, Helios combines real-time interaction with scientific visualization to provide an engaging learning experience.

Users can explore the relationship between magnetic fields, plasma, and energy through interactive controls, animated magnetic field lines, live simulation metrics, and a real-time energy graph. Rather than presenting static information, Helios encourages hands-on exploration, helping users better understand the physical principles governing the Sun's plasma environment.

Built using **HTML, CSS, and JavaScript**, Helios is based on core concepts from electromagnetism and plasma physics, including the Lorentz force, magnetic pressure, magnetic energy density, and magnetic flux. The project presents these principles through simplified physical models and interactive visualization, making them easier to understand while remaining scientifically grounded. It is intended as an educational simulation and does not perform full magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) numerical modeling.

Helios aims to bridge the gap between theoretical physics and interactive learning by transforming complex scientific concepts into an intuitive and visually immersive experience.

title: physics that keeps jupiters asteriods in place
author: PURVA KAKDE
tagline: five points in space where gravity balances some hold forever , some don't.
description:
Libration — Real-Time Simulation of the Circular Restricted Three-Body Problem
What it is
A real-time physics simulation of the five Lagrange points — the equilibrium locations created when two massive bodies orbit a common center of mass. These aren't fictional; they're the same points that hold Jupiter's ~10,000 known Trojan asteroids in place and the same point (Sun–Earth L2) where JWST currently sits, station-keeping with almost no fuel.
How it works
The simulation integrates the actual Circular Restricted Three-Body Problem equations of motion — no shortcuts, no pre-baked animation:
Code   x'' - 2y' = ∂Ω/∂x
           y'' + 2x' = ∂Ω/∂y 
where Ω is the Jacobi effective potential combining gravity from both bodies with the centrifugal effect of the rotating frame. Every one of ~1,800 particles is stepped forward with 4th-order Runge-Kutta integration, frame by frame. The five Lagrange points themselves are found by numerically solving for the roots of this potential, not hardcoded coordinates. The glowing "terrain" you see rendered underneath is that same potential, sampled on a grid — it's data, not a background image.
Why it's cool
You can watch stability and instability happen, not just be told about them. Particles seeded at L4/L5 librate in stable tadpole orbits indefinitely — a direct, visual proof of Routh's stability criterion (μ < 0.0385). Particles seeded at L1, L2, and L3 slowly drift away over the course of the simulation, because those points are genuine saddle points of the potential — the instability is computed, not scripted.
One click changes your entire frame of reference. Toggling rotating ↔ inertial frame is just a rotation matrix applied to the same underlying physics — in one view the Lagrange points sit frozen while particles swarm around them; in the other, everything visibly orbits together. It's the clearest possible demonstration that "stationary" and "orbiting" are just two descriptions of the same motion.
It's the real mechanism behind real spacecraft and real asteroid populations — this isn't an abstract toy system, it's the exact math NASA uses to plan Lagrange-point missions.
Built entirely in vanilla JavaScript and Canvas2D — no physics engine, no shader tricks, just the equations themselves running at 60fps.

title: Blackhole simulation
author: Ganesh Karanth
tagline: Blackhole- Where the Universe hides it secrets
description:
This project is a scientifically inspired, real-time black hole simulation that demonstrates how gravity bends space, time, and light. Built using modern programming techniques and mathematical models based on Einstein's General Theory of Relativity, the simulation visualizes key black hole phenomena such as gravitational lensing, the event horizon, photon trajectories, and the accretion disk.

Unlike simple animations, the simulation calculates the behavior of light rays as they pass near the black hole, creating realistic visual distortions similar to those predicted by physics. Users can freely explore the scene, adjust parameters such as black hole mass and camera position, and observe how spacetime curvature changes the appearance of surrounding stars and matter.

The project combines computer graphics, physics, and mathematics to make one of the universe's most fascinating objects easier to understand. It serves as both an educational tool and an interactive visualization, helping students, researchers, and space enthusiasts experience the extraordinary effects of extreme gravity in an engaging and immersive way.

title: Hawking Radiation Lab: A Interactive Black Hole Evaporation Simulator
author: Briji Issac
tagline: Explore Hawking radiation, black hole thermodynamics, and quantum tunneling through real-time scientific simulation and GPU-accelerated visualization.
description:
Hawking Radiation Lab is a research-oriented scientific visualization platform designed for students studying general relativity, quantum field theory in curved spacetime, and black hole thermodynamics. The application combines a validated Python-based physics engine with an interactive WebGL/Three.js visualization environment to simulate Hawking radiation and black hole evaporation in real time.

The platform supports Schwarzschild, Kerr, and Reissner–Nordström black holes, computing Hawking temperature, Bekenstein–Hawking entropy, horizon geometry, blackbody emission, greybody corrections, stochastic particle emission, adaptive numerical integration, and black hole lifetime estimation. Multiple particle species—including photons, neutrinos, electrons, positrons, and higher-energy particles—are emitted according to semiclassical physics.

A modern GPU-accelerated visualization engine renders event horizons, accretion disks, photon spheres, gravitational lensing effects, and Hawking radiation particle systems while providing interactive controls, live scientific plots, data export, parameter editing, comparison mode, and comprehensive educational annotations. The modular architecture, testing framework, and extensible plugin design make the project suitable for advanced education, research demonstrations, and future expansion into more complex relativistic simulations.

title: Wesker: A Real-Time Sagittarius A*
author: Aryan Amrute
tagline: A real-time simulation of Sagittarius A*, our galaxy's own black hole, ray-traced live inside Unreal Engine 5.8 using the actual Kerr geodesic equations — not a script, an interactive simulation you can fly through.
description:
Wesker is a real-time simulation of Sagittarius A*, the supermassive black hole at the center of our own galaxy . More specifically,  the one the Event Horizon Telescope actually photographed in May 2022, about 27,000 light-years from Earth. It runs entirely inside Unreal Engine 5.8.

Every frame, a custom HLSL shader fires a ray backwards from the camera for roughly a million pixels, then bends each one by numerically integrating the real Kerr geodesic equations (RK4 integration, up to 300 steps per ray) as the spinning black hole solution, since real black holes spin. Nothing in the resulting image is hand-drawn: the photon ring, the warped image of the disk's far side arcing over the shadow, and the brightness asymmetry across the disk all emerge directly from the ray-bending math.

Every frame also computes real physics live: the event horizon and innermost stable orbit as actual functions of spin, a combined gravitational-redshift-and-Doppler "g factor" that sets both brightness (∝ g⁴) and color shift per ray, and a blackbody temperature-to-color mapping for the disk. A HUD reports distance, redshift, and time dilation as you fly, recalculated continuously.

Unlike Interstellar's Gargantua, which used a fictional near-maximal spin chosen for the plot, Sgr A*'s real spin has never been precisely measured — so spin here is treated honestly, as a tunable illustration rather than a settled number.

Approximations are disclosed, not hidden: the disk is idealized as thin and stable, where the real EHT image is patchy and changes shape within minutes (a full orbit this close takes only ~30 minutes); turbulence is procedural noise, not fluid dynamics; background stars aren't redshifted.

Mass, spin, size, and dust are live parameters so drag spin up and watch the horizon's shadow visibly distort into the D-shape frame dragging predicts.

Finally, the shader renders a mathematically exaggerated violet quantum fringe at the event horizon boundary to represent Hawking radiation while acknowledging the quantum emissions that, though physically far too cold to see (~1.5e-14 K), connect general relativity to quantum thermodynamics.

title: Spaghettification (Blackhole)
author: Neha Veer
tagline: Getting into a new world maybe.
description:
Spaghettification, famously popularized by Stephen Hawking, is the process where extreme gravitational tidal forces near a black hole stretch an object vertically and compress it horizontally. This causes the object to elongate into a long, thin string-like shape—similar to a piece of spaghetti.
Spaghettification is caused by extreme tidal forces. The formula to calculate this tidal acceleration (\(\Delta a\)) across an object's length is derived from Newtonian gravity as:\(\Delta a\approx \frac{2GML}{R^{3}}\)

title: A Majestic Blackhole Simulation
author: Divya Jaiswal
tagline: A real-time black hole simulation built using Python and Taichi.
description:
The project visualizes an accretion disk with thousands of particles orbiting a black hole, creating smooth gravitational motion inspired by real astrophysical phenomena.

Unlike a scientifically accurate simulator, this project focuses on creating a calm, cinematic, and visually appealing representation of a black hole. Dynamic particle motion, glowing trails, and color transitions combine to create an immersive cosmic experience while remaining lightweight enough to run on CPU-only systems.

## Features
- Python + Taichi
- CPU-friendly simulation
- Particle-based accretion disk
- Dynamic colors and glow
- Smooth gravitational motion

title: BHLab
author: Meiree
tagline: A real-time black hole simulator in one Python file: watch gravity bend light, throw stars into the hole, and even fly through the event horizon yourself.
description:
BHLab is a 3D black hole simulator that runs in real time. The whole project is a single Python file, and all the heavy math runs on the GPU using the Taichi library.
The black hole is not a drawing. For every pixel on the screen, the simulator shoots a ray of light and bends it with the same rule gravity uses. Rays that get too close never come back. And that is exactly what creates the black shadow, the thin ring of light around it, and the strangely twisted glowing disk. The disk's colors are real physics too. The gas coming toward you looks brighter and bluer, and the gas moving away looks dimmer and redder.
It is also fun to play with. You can throw planets and stars at the hole and watch them slowly spiral in. You can build a black hole from a dying star, crash two black holes together, tear a giant star apart, or make the hole evaporate. You can load real black holes like M87*, with their true mass and spin. And at the end, you can dive inside, while an info box explains the physics on screen, with the real equations.

title: Binary Blackhole
author: Sasikumar
tagline: A real-time, GPU-accelerated simulation of binary black hole coalescence and accretion disk dynamics
description:
Binary Black Hole Dance is a high-performance, real-time physical simulation of a binary black hole system undergoing inspiral, coalescence, and remnant formation, surrounded by a dynamic accretion disk. Built using the Taichi Lang framework, the simulation executes fully parallelized on the GPU, enabling the simultaneous tracking and rendering of 850,000 accretion disk particles.

The orbital dynamics of the binary system incorporate first-order Post-Newtonian (1PN) corrections to model Schwarzschild-like precession (periastron advance), combined with a tangential drag force that mimics Peters (1964) gravitational wave energy and angular momentum loss. When the separation drops below the combined event horizon threshold, the black holes merge into a single remnant black hole. This process conserves linear momentum while converting 5% of the total system mass-energy into an expanding gravitational wave front.

The surrounding accretion disk consists of massless test particles executing chaotic orbits within the dynamic gravitational potential of the binary. Gas particles that cross the event horizon are absorbed, while escaped particles are recycled at the boundaries with Keplerian velocities. To visually capture the thermodynamics of the disk, particle speeds are translated to temperatures via the virial theorem and mapped to a thermal blackbody colormap. Relativistic visual features—such as the photon sphere (rendered as a glowing ring) and event horizon silhouettes—are fully integrated, offering a visually stunning, physically informed representation of binary black hole physics.

title: Star-to-Singularity
author: Daiwik D
tagline: A journey from a star to singularity , when light chooses the dark path.
description:
Star to Singularity is an interactive scientific simulation that visualizes the complete life cycle of a massive star, from its birth as a main-sequence star to its dramatic death in a core-collapse supernova and its final transformation into a black hole. The simulation combines astrophysical principles with real-time rendering to demonstrate how stellar evolution depends on mass, gravity, nuclear fusion, and gravitational collapse.

The project features a smooth transition between evolutionary phases, physically inspired supernova effects, a dynamically generated accretion disk, a photon ring, Doppler beaming, gravitational redshift, and gravitational lensing around the black hole. A live information panel displays important physical quantities such as stellar mass, temperature, luminosity, Schwarzschild radius, photon sphere radius, and accretion properties, helping users connect the visual effects with the underlying physics.

The goal of this project is to make complex astrophysical concepts intuitive and engaging through interactive visualization. By combining scientific accuracy with real-time graphics, Star to Singularity serves as an educational tool that allows students to explore the fascinating evolution of some of the universe's most extreme objects.

title: Binary Neutron Star Inspiral & Kilonova
author: Prakhar Dubey
tagline: A real-time GPU-accelerated simulation of binary neutron star orbital decay, relativistic gravitational lensing, synchrotron pulsar jets, and a kilonova merger shockwave.
description:
This project is a high-performance, real-time astrophysical simulation built using Python and the Taichi high-performance GPU programming language. It models the final inspiral and explosive merger of a binary neutron star system—the exact cosmic event responsible for generating gravitational waves and forging the universe's heaviest elements, such as gold and platinum.

How it works: The simulation computes gravitational n-body orbital mechanics with inspiral damping to simulate orbital decay due to gravitational wave radiation. It features a Doppler-boosted plasma accretion disk where particle luminosity and frequency shift dynamically based on their velocity vector relative to the camera. Synchrotron pulsar jets sweep through space from the magnetic poles of the spinning neutron stars. Crucially, a custom post-processing shader calculates real-time relativistic gravitational lensing, physically bending and distorting the coordinates of background stars and accretion plasma around the gravity wells of the massive stellar cores.

Why it's cool: When the orbital separation reaches critical threshold, the system dynamically transitions into a "Kilonova" state. The binary stars collapse into a single hyper-massive magnetar, releasing a blinding gamma-ray overexposure flash and propagating a glowing, volumetric shockwave ring across the starfield. It bridges complex theoretical astrophysics with cinematic, real-time interactive rendering at 160+ FPS.

title: Real time VOID
author: V
tagline: Experience the mind-bending physics of curved spacetime with an interactive, mathematically exact black hole ray-tracer that runs in real-time.
description:
Built in Python and Taichi, this GPU-accelerated sandbox visualizes a dynamic Kerr black hole complete with a super-heated accretion disk, X-ray corona, and relativistic polar jets. Instead of using standard 3D polygons, it operates as a relativistic ray-tracer—firing virtual photons backwards from your camera and calculating their exact paths through curved spacetime using the Kerr-Schild metric.

The engine actively computes true physical phenomena like gravitational redshift, relativistic Doppler beaming, and blackbody thermodynamics. A Temporal Anti-Aliasing (TAA) pipeline then processes the raw math into a razor-sharp, photorealistic image. It is a mathematically rigorous astrophysics simulation where you can adjust the spin and watch exact gravitational lensing warp a procedurally generated Milky Way at 60 FPS.

Controls
0:SCHWARZSCHILD and 1:KERR (in the toggle bar)
Mouse (Left Click + Drag): Orbit the camera around the black hole.
Mouse Wheel (or Z / X): Zoom in and out.
Shift (Hold): Move, zoom, and orbit faster.
Spacebar: Pause or resume simulation time.
[ / ] or - / =: Decrease or increase the speed of time.
1 - 5: Instantly snap to cinematic camera presets.
6: Start an automatic cinematic tour.

Esc: Quit the simulation.

title: X-ray Accreting Pulsar Engine
author: Abeer
tagline: Houston, we have plasma.
description:
Have you ever wondered what it looks like when a dead star's heart gets angry? Welcome to the cosmic violence simulator nobody knew they needed where you get first row seats to the most destructive tantrum in the universe!


What are pulsars?
Pulsars are highly magnetized, rapidly rotating neutron stars, and are categorized by rotation-powered, accretion-powered, or magnetar types based on how they generate energy. 
They are also classified by their spin speed, which separates younger, slower ordinary pulsars from highly accelerated, "recycled" millisecond pulsars. Depending on their energy output, these cosmic objects primarily broadcast their beams across radio, X-ray, or gamma-ray wavelengths.

- The simulation -
This simulation models an X-ray binary accretion system (a neutron star or compact object pulling material in from a companion star through an accretion disk)
The material spirals inward, gets heated to insane temperatures, and gets ejected as relativistic jets shooting perpendicular to the disk. The jets get squeezed and collimated by magnetic fields, creating those narrow beams you see.
The plasma jets (the two beams you see): relativistic plasma (electrons and positrons) that produce synchrotron radiation in magnetic fields – X-rays, radio, infrared, optical. We call them X-ray binaries because X-rays are the brightest part, the jets are plasma but the X-rays are just their emission.
The accretion disk (the orange spiral): material from a companion star spiraling into the neutron star at the center. As it falls in, friction heats it up and gravity tears it apart, which powers the entire system and launches the jets. 
 

- The reason behind the sim -
I built this to visualize that specific scenario because it's one of the most violent and energetic processes in space, and frankly, it's beautiful when you can actually see it happen. 
The main thing I wanted to showcase is that accretion-ejection balance, how material falls in, releases gravitational energy, and the system throws half of it back out as jets. That's what powers X-ray binaries and other extreme systems. 
Some real life examples of an X-ray accreting pulsar would be the Her X-1, Cen X-3, Vela X-1, etc.


The Science behind the sim
- Actually accurate -
Keplerian orbital mechanics - material closer to the center moves faster
Logarithmic spiral structure in the disk - real density wave patterns
Magnetic pinch instability - keeps jets collimated instead of exploding sideways
Relativistic beaming - particles moving toward you appear brighter (Lorentz effect)
Particle aging and recycling - continuous system dynamics
Proper 3D-to-2D projection with perspective and distance
Additive light blending - realistic photon accumulation and glow
Force balance between gravity pulling inward and jets pushing outward
- Simplified for Performance -
Magnetic field is a sine-wave pattern, not a full 3D MHD simulation
Temperature is visual only - real disks reach 10 million Kelvin
No pair production (real jets are more energetic at that scale)
Softening parameter prevents math errors near center
Uniform particle mass (reality has electrons and ions)
Uniform viscosity (real disks have turbulent variation)
Jets are too smooth (real systems like Her X-1 have chaotic jets)

How real is this?
This simulation captures the core physics of how X-ray binaries actually work.
The accretion-ejection mechanism is real, the orbital mechanics are legit, and the magnetic confinement is based on actual phenomena we observe. But it's cleaner than reality. 
Real systems like Her X-1 and Cen X-3 are messier, and chaotic with turbulent jets driven by brutal surface magnetic fields. This is a more simplified but scientifically grounded visualization of what's happening. 


- Simulation controls -
Jet Velocity (0.1–5.0): How fast particles escape outward
Magnetic Pinch (0.1–5.0): How tightly fields squeeze the jets
Relativistic Beaming (0.0–3.0): Doppler brightening effect
Time Speed (0.1–5.0): Simulation speed
Active Particles (5K–15K): Particle count (more = smoother but slower)
Friction/Damping (0.0–1.0): Plasma resistance
Color Mode (0–2): Temperature, Age, or Velocity coloring 
Space bar — Pause and resume the simulation
R key — Reset everything back to default
You can adjust these parameters using the sliders as per your preference! 

Thank you for reading, hoping you like it :)

title: Cosmic Encounter: Ultra realistic Self-Gravitating Galaxy Collision
author: Caroline Azevedo
tagline: Two galaxies with 800,000 self-gravitating stars and gas particles collide in real time, complete with star formation and gravitational lensing.
description:
Cosmic Encounter is a real-time astrophysics simulation built with Taichi that models the collision of two spiral galaxies, one barred, one unbarred, each with its own NFW dark-matter halo, stellar disk, and gas disk. Unlike simple particle-pulled-toward-a-point setups, this simulation solves true self-gravity: a particle-mesh method deposits mass onto a 128³ grid every couple of frames and solves the Poisson equation with a Jacobi relaxation solver to compute the gravitational potential the whole system generates on itself, not just from the two central black-hole-like cores.
Gas particles behave like an actual fluid, following a polytropic equation of state with pressure gradients computed directly on the grid. When gas gets compressed densely enough, like in the shockfront of the collision, it heats up and, with a bit of randomness, spontaneously converts into young stars, visualized as bright blue points bursting to life exactly where the galaxies smash together. A screen-space gravitational lensing pass then bends light around both galactic cores, distorting the final image the way mass genuinely warps spacetime.
You can orbit and zoom the camera, speed up or slow down time, and pull particles around with your mouse to interact directly with the simulation. A "Learn Mode" toggle switches to a scientific heatmap showing real-time acceleration magnitude across all 800,000 particles alongside live technical telemetry (grid resolution, particle count, update cadence), making it as much an educational tool as a visual centerpiece.

title: Boomerang Nebula–Black Hole Interaction Simulator

description:

The project explores a hypothetical scenario in which a central black hole resides within the Boomerang Nebula, producing an evolving environment shaped by gravity, accretion processes, bipolar relativistic jets, and expanding nebular gas.

Rather than serving as a numerically exact astrophysical solver, the simulation is science-inspired, capturing the essential physical behavior of these phenomena while maintaining the performance required for real-time visualization. The simulation models slowly evolving gas clouds using a particle-based approach, allowing the nebula to dynamically respond to the gravitational influence of the black hole and energetic jet outflows. The resulting interaction produces an immersive three-dimensional environment that emphasizes both scientific intuition and visual fidelity.

title: life after him
author: preet bodke
tagline: its shiny, purple, pretty, diva, beautiful, and lonely
description:
When I ran this Kerr Black Hole simulation, I sorta realized I wasn’t only rendering astrophysics, I was also staring at a mirror of my own life.  

The accretion disk that radiant sweeping band of purple starlight is basically the momentum of my days. It is still pretty, still shiny ,and I’m moving ahead, spinning with the charge of my ambitions and what comes next. From the outside perspective the halo looks mesmerizing. I am still glowing, still carrying the beautiful dust of my own cosmos, and I keep painting the dark canvas of space with sweeping colorful strokes.  

But right at the center of all that movement there’s the singularity. It is a heavy quiet abyss left behind by Him. It behaves like a gravitational pit of loneliness, anchored right there, in the middle of my glowing schedule. I can be surrounded by the lovely spinning stars of my life, and still feel that inescapable silent pull of empty space. It is a darkness so dense that even the brightest moments, the most triumphant ones, sometimes seem to bend straight into it, like swallowed by the sheer weight of his absence.  
Still, this is the paradox of my grief, and also the heart vibe of my Simathon submission. The void is total, but it does not erase the light. The light hasnt vanished; it’s just learning how to live around the immense gravity of this loss. My life, kind of like the vibrant starlight in this simulation, keeps dancing along the edge of the event horizon—brilliant and defiant, even when the center feels impossibly lonely.

title: The Formation of a Black Hole in a nutshell!
author: s
tagline: Explore stellar collapse using an interactive 3D simulator that visualizes black hole formation stage by stage!
description:
What it is: a compact interactive simulator that demonstrates how a massive star collapses into a black hole. It stages the process (stable star → core collapse → event horizon → accretion disk → tidal disruption → jets) and visualizes each step with simple 3D graphics with explaination to aid understanding.

How it works: the app is built with React and Three.js (r3f). A stage-driven controller updates scene parameters (object sizes, ring geometry, particle count, jet visibility) while lightweight physics helpers compute horizon distance, disk temperature, and simple velocity/dilation metrics. The accretion disk is an instanced particle system with color, size, and spiral motion to suggest heating and inflow. Camera modes let you follow a guided educational view or freely explore. A cinematic toggle adjusts visual fidelity for performance.

Why it’s cool: Turning abstract astrophysics into an simplified, explorable experience, students and curious users can see cause-and-effect, tweak perspectives, and watch dynamic structures emerge, stage by stage. This stage by stage structure also aids comprehension as it's easy to follow. Hence, it can be used as a teaching tool suited for most ages, via its visually engaging demo.

title: Gravitational Lensing
author: Karthiazheni
tagline: What happens when 'G' dominates 'c'
description:
Gravitational lensing is spacetime's optical illusion. A black hole's gravity is so intense it bends the very fabric of space, warping the path of light that passes nearby, like a cosmic magnifying glass.

 Light from stars behind the black hole gets stretched, smeared, and sometimes duplicated into multiple images. A Perfect alignment creates an "Einstein ring", a full circle of light. Around black holes, it goes further: photons can orbit the event horizon before escaping, forming a glowing "photon ring." It's why black holes don't look like empty voids in space but rather look like warped, glowing halos, bending the universe around them.

title: kerr-neumann metric blackhole
author: Bala venkat
tagline: Bending Light, Not the Rules: A Real-Time Hamiltonian Raytracer for Kerr-Newman Spacetime.
description:
Most hackathon black holes are optical illusions—standard 3D rasterization or noise textures faking spatial warping. We threw that out. This project is a raw, numerical physics engine simulating the literal tensor geometry of spacetime.
The Hamiltonian Difference
Instead of using simplified Newtonian approximations, we modeled the universe using Hamiltonian Mechanics. Our engine calculates the Hamiltonian energy equation of a massless photon traveling through a Kerr-Newman metric (a charged, spinning black hole). By numerically solving Hamilton’s equations of motion—calculating the derivatives of momentum and position at every step—we generate the true, physical geodesic paths of light falling into the gravity well.
Computational Prowess
Solving continuous tensor calculus per-pixel is computationally massive. To render this interactively without melting the GPU, we wrote a custom 4th-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4) integrator directly in GLSL, optimized with:
•	Adaptive Ray-Stepping: Dynamically scales step size based on horizon proximity, saving immense computing power in empty space.
•	Coordinate Singularity Management: The math is so accurate it naturally encounters real-world division-by-zero singularities at Boyer-Lindquist poles, patched via spatial clamping for rendering stability.
•	Dithered Volumetrics: High-frequency ray jitter shatters moiré banding, allowing us to drop loop counts while delivering cinematic, artifact-free plasma.
The result is a fully interactive simulation where gravitational lensing and frame-dragging are physically simulated, proving you don't need to fake physics to make it beautiful.

title: F16 flight simulator
author: Hridayesh Khetan
tagline: A real time, 17-state nonlinear mathematical simulation based of the F-16 model publicly released by NASA.
description:
This is a real time, 17-state nonlinear flight dynamics simulator of the F 16 aircraft using the publicly available f16 model published in 1979 by NASA and Caltech . 

https://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/projects/afosr95-vehicles/models/f16/ 
https://ntrs.nasa.gov/citations/19770009539

It utilizes Python, specifically Taichi and numpy in the backend to simulate complex physics, including a layered atmospheric model that uses data from the U.S. Standard Atmosphere model 1976 for accurate information on temperature ,pressure, local air density and lapse rate.
The simulator runs a 200 Hz physics engine in parallel with a 60 FPS Pygame visualization suite, enabling the execution of complex aerodynamic manoeuvres without the need of dedicated GPU.

The simulation uses Newton's laws of motion in the rotating frame of the aircraft and thus takes account of the Coriolis acceleration as well as rotational acceleration from the aircraft's various manoeuvres. 
Furthermore, fluid mechanics is also incorporated by simulating the drag and lift the F16 receives in its various manoeuvres such as the Kvochur bell .
Lift,Drag and Sideforce are all computed using non dimensional coefficients that are evaluated as functions of Angle of Attack alpha’’, Sideslip “beta’”, and control deflections.
A 4 dimensional Quaternion unit is used for tracking the spatial orientation of the aircraft without running into gimbal lock errors at +-90 degree pitch.

Lastly, all the non linear differential equations are resolved mathematically using a 4th-order Runge Kutta (RK4) numerical integration algorithm which evaluates the derivatives at four distinct intermediate stages over a time step delta.

Controls:
    Left-drag   : rotate the 3D camera
    Scroll      : zoom the 3D camera
    Click a button in the control panel to fly a manoeuvre

All sound effects are generated by the code with no files required for download other than the code.

title: Einstein's Trampoline
author: Anusha Mahapatra
tagline: Where general relativity meets procedural art.
description:
Einstein’s Trampoline is a humble attempt at playing with General Relativity without melting my GPU. It is a simple, interactive Kerr black hole toy that integrates null geodesics in Boyer-Lindquist coordinates using a standard Runge-Kutta 4 solver. It lets you bend light rays with your cursor, watch space get twisted by the Lense-Thirring effect, and observe virtual Hawking pairs boil near the horizon. I also wrote a procedural synthesizer to play some cozy, Interstellar-style organ chords in the background. It's not a supercomputer simulation, but it's a fun way to watch math come to life!
Physics Employed in the Simulation:
Kerr Spacetime Geometry: Models a rotating black hole of Mass $M=1.0$ and Spin parameter $a^*=0.98$. It defines the inner event horizon radius ($r_+ = M + \sqrt{M^2 - a^2}$) where the coordinate speed of light asymptotically goes to zero relative to distant observers.
Boyer-Lindquist RK4 Geodesics: Light ray trajectories (null geodesics) are computed by solving the canonical equations of motion in Boyer-Lindquist coordinates using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) numerical integrator, factoring in the metric tensor coefficients ($g_{rr}, g_{\theta\theta}, g_{\phi\phi}, g_{tt}, g_{t\phi}$).
Lense-Thirring Effect (Frame Dragging): Simulates the gravitomagnetic precession where space itself is dragged in the direction of the black hole's rotation. The angular velocity of the dragging is calculated as $\omega = 2aM/r^3$ and applied directly to the accretion disk flow, the photon ring, and Hawking pairs.
Relativistic Doppler Beaming: Accretion disk brightness is modulated by the line-of-sight velocity of the orbiting plasma. The approaching side (rotating toward the camera) is relativistically beamed (brighter and blue-shifted), while the receding side is dimmed and red-shifted.
Einstein Lensing & Chromatic Aberration: Simulates primary and secondary gravitational lensing which warps background stars and projects the accretion disk behind the shadow as lensed arcs above and below the horizon. The Red, Green, and Blue light channels are lensed at slightly different strengths ($1.01\times, 1.00\times, 0.99\times$) to show visual chromatic dispersion during camera movement.
Semiclassical Hawking Radiation: Models pair production fluctuations near the horizon boundary. Entangled virtual pairs split: infalling particles plunge into the singularity, and escaping particles are swept by frame-dragging before escaping the gravity well.
Why do I think it's cool?.......
Interactive Relativistic Spacetime: Unlike static or pre-rendered black hole visualizations, this project is fully interactive. The cursor acts as a photon probe, tracing real-time Kerr geodesics, while left-clicking applies a gravity pull to physically swirl the plasma.
60 FPS GPU Physics: Powered by Taichi, the Python code compiles directly to Vulkan GPU kernels at startup, allowing you to run 70,000 accretion particles and 15,000 photon ring orbits smoothly in real-time.
Procedural Synth Engine: The soundtrack contains no audio files. The Interstellar-style pipe organ chords and space-time ticking are synthesized programmatically using mathematical waveforms and LFOs.
Authentic Physics Modeling: Incorporates relativistic Doppler beaming (asymmetrical brightness), Lense-Thirring frame-dragging (rotational space twist), Einstein lensing, and chromatic aberration dispersion (light channel splitting during movement).
Zero Bloat, Standalone File: The entire graphics engine, physics integrator, user controls, and sound synthesizer live in a single standalone Python script (sim.py).

title: Gargantua
author: Gautam Ganesh
tagline: A black hole with an accretion disk
description:
What makes this simulation extraordinary is that it isn't just a generic 3D animation—it's a mathematically rigorous, real-time physics engine rendering true general relativity.

Instead of faking the movement of the accretion disk, every single one of the 2,500 particles is independently integrated using a 4th-order Runge-Kutta solver to follow an exact equatorial geodesic through the curved space-time of a Schwarzschild metric.

The visual effects are equally grounded in astrophysics. The colors aren't arbitrary; they map directly to the Novikov-Thorne thermal profile, meaning the outer disk glows a cool, faint orange, while the Innermost Stable Circular Orbit (ISCO) burns a brilliant white-hot. As the particles whip around the black hole at relativistic speeds approaching $c$, the code calculates proper Doppler beaming, causing the side of the disk moving toward the camera to appear exponentially brighter than the receding side.

Most impressively, the optical warping isn't a post-processing trick. The GPU actively projects and bends the light from both the accretion disk and the background starfield using the exact Schwarzschild gravitational lens equations. This creates the geometrically perfect $5.196M$ circular shadow and the iconic "halo" effect as light from the far side of the disk is gravitationally dragged over the poles, all computed interactively in 3D space!

title: Black Hole Simulation

This project bridges the gap between abstract mathematics and visual art, offering a dynamic, immersive window into the extreme environment surrounding a black hole.

author: Bhoumik

description:
AREPO is a high-performance, GPU-accelerated astrophysics simulator that models and visualizes Kerr black hole magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) accretion disks. Built using Python 3.12 and Taichi 1.7.4 (CUDA backend), it runs interactive, real-time simulations of thousands of particles orbiting under extreme relativistic conditions.

Key Features:
Relativistic Physics Engine: Simulates particle dynamics under Paczynski-Wiita gravity, Lense-Thirring frame dragging, Shakura-Sunyaev alpha-viscosity, and magnetic pressure gradient forces, with a stochastic proxy for magnetorotational instability (MRI) turbulence.
Unified Raytracer: Implements volumetric JIT-compiled raymarching to capture gravitational lensing (light-bending), Doppler beaming, gravitational redshift, and ergosphere/relativistic jet glow.
Holographic Glass UI: Features an overlay control panel rendering real-time telemetry, visual analysis modes (density, velocity, temperature, B-field magnitude, plasma-beta, camera controls, physics parameter tuning, and a holographic discovery fact card.
Performance Optimized: Uses NumPy-based bulk transfers to eliminate host-device communication latency, enabling smooth JIT compilation and frame rates.

title: Ultimate Spectral Chaos: N-Body Problem, Black Hole Merger
author: Yashoda Bajpai Pathangay
tagline: Disrupt a two body system with YOUR black hole(s) and CONTROL the forces ripping apart stars
description:
Ultimate Spectral Chaos is an interactive real-time 3D gravitational field simulator built with Python and Taichi. It combines an N-body gravity solver with GPU-accelerated particle rendering to visualize how gravitational fields evolve through orbital motion. Rather than using scripted animations, every orbit, resonance, capture event, and chaotic interaction emerges naturally from the underlying physics.

YOU can interact with it by creating your OWN black holes. Keep an eye on the black hole counter in the bottom left as there is no upper limit till your laptop crashes.

The simulation begins with a stable binary black hole system. New black holes can be added to reshape the gravitational field, while 1,000 massless tracer particles continuously reveal orbital paths, gravitational streams, resonances, and chaotic regions. Two visualization modes present the same physics in different ways: Light Mode emphasizes clarity to see the physics at work more clearly through the motion of the particles and a simpler black hole model, while Heavy Mode renders visually cohesive black holes with event horizons, rotating accretion disks, gravitational lensing and a central view of the spaghettification process.

Featuring…
> Real-time 3D N-body gravity simulation
> Stable binary black hole system >at the start
> Long-term orbital rotational mechanics
> 1,000 GPU-accelerated tracer particles
> Emergent orbital paths, resonances, and chaotic dynamics
> Light and Heavy rendering modes (preventing overheating)
> Accretion disks and approximate gravitational lensing
> Interactive 3D orbit camera
> Adjustable simulation speed and visual quality
> Detailed physics control panel
> Optimized GPU implementation using Taichi

Created using Python, Taichi, Numpy!!

Ultimate Spectral Chaos was developed as a fun scientific visual to demonstrate the orbital mechanics of combining black holes and the forces of attraction of the particles around it. I hope you liked the simulation :)

*If Heavy Mode is too laggy, switch to Light Mode, however, the visual quality will be heavily compromised while the physics of the particles will still be rendered accurately.

title: Blackhole Coalescence Simulation
author: Nishika Bugatha
tagline: Why have 1 when you can have 2
description:
This project is an interactive visualization of two black holes merging, inspired by the binary black hole merger animations released by LIGO (https://www.ligo.caltech.edu/video/ligo20160211v3) after the first direct detection of gravitational waves. The goal was to recreate the different stages of a black hole coalescence while making the physics intuitive and visually engaging.

The simulation begins with two black holes orbiting each other in an inspiral phase. As they lose energy, they move closer together, eventually forming a common apparent horizon before relaxing into a single rotating (Kerr) black hole. The motion is driven by a simplified physics model based on orbital decay and merger dynamics.

To better illustrate the behaviour of spacetime, the simulation includes a deforming spacetime field, geodesic tracer particles, and gravitational lensing effects that evolve alongside the black holes. These visual elements are synchronized with the same underlying physics so that the merger appears as one continuous process rather than a series of scripted animations.

The project was built using React, Three.js, React Three Fiber, and custom GLSL shaders to achieve real-time rendering directly in the browser. The focus was on balancing scientific inspiration with performance so that the simulation remains interactive while conveying the key stages of a black hole merger.

title: Black Holes.
author: Aarushi
tagline: A point of no return.
description:
This project is an interactive, real-time black hole simulation built in Python using the Taichi GPU framework. It delivers a cinematic visual of a glowing accretion disk and event horizon while remaining fully playable and responsive on a standard PC.

The simulation calculates the orbital mechanics of 50,000 individual gas particles using Paczyński-Wiita gravity, accurately modelling the Innermost Stable Circular Orbit (ISCO). 

It bridges the gap between raw astrophysics and beautiful creative coding. You can dynamically orbit the camera, alter time dilation, toggle relativistic Doppler beaming, and spawn tidal disruption events in real-time.

title: QUASAR – Quantum Wave Analysis & Slit Simulation for Advanced Research
author: ananyaraju.canraju06
tagline: Where Quantum Theory Meets Interactive Reality.
description:
QUASAR is an interactive simulation platform that recreates Young's Double-Slit Experiment using real-time computational physics. Developed with Python, Taichi, and scientific visualization libraries, the simulator numerically solves the 2D scalar wave equation to generate physically accurate diffraction and interference patterns while demonstrating fundamental quantum mechanics concepts such as wave–particle duality, single-particle accumulation, and measurement-induced decoherence.

The platform enables users to modify experimental parameters,including wavelength, slit width, slit separation, source characteristics, and detector settings—in real time while visualizing the resulting interference behavior through multiple scientific and educational views. By combining GPU-accelerated numerical computation with an intuitive interactive interface, QUASAR transforms one of physics' most iconic experiments into an engaging educational and research tool.

title: Black hole Simulation With A Geodesic Path Analyser And More
author: Nirva Desai
tagline: Accuracy is key 😉
description:
Project Title: Celestia — GR Masterpiece EditionProject OverviewCelestia is an advanced, real-time, interactive General Relativity (GR) simulator designed to visualize the extreme physics of spinning (Kerr) black holes. Operating right inside a custom, real-time web environment, the application bridges the gap between complex mathematical astrophysics and high-performance front-end design, delivering movie-quality visuals at a physical level of accuracy.Scientific & Physics Accuracy (The Black Hole Engine)This simulator actively computes light paths (geodesics) through warped spacetime using custom fragment shaders. Key physics features include:Kerr Spacetime Frame-Dragging: Simulates the gravitational physics of a rotating black hole, resulting in the iconic asymmetric flattening and distortion of the central event horizon shadow.Relativistic Doppler Beaming & Redshift: Simulates physical light frequency changes. Gas in the accretion disk rushing toward the observer undergoes extreme blueshifting to appear blindingly white-hot, while gas moving away experiences deep gravitational redshift and dimming.Gravitational Lensing & Einstein Rings: Employs intense coordinate-warping algorithms near the photon sphere regime to realistically stretch and warp background starfields into sweeping, concentric Einstein rings.Dual-Ring Accretion Lensing: Accurately renders the path of light traveling from behind the horizon, creating the path-warped top and bottom loops of the accretion disk.Creative Engineering & UI FeaturesThe visual simulator is wrapped in a highly polished, interactive dashboard built for data transparency and user experimentation:Real-Time Parametric Sandbox: Features interactive control sliders allowing users to dynamically modify variables on the fly, including black hole spin parameter ($a$), observer inclination angle, fields of view (FOV), and exposure.Astrophysical Simulation Presets: Equipped with quick-load configurations for famous cosmic bodies, including M87*, Sagittarius A*, and the cinematic Gargantua.Live Geodesic Path Analyzer: An integrated 2D subsystem visualization tracking equatorial null geodesics, plotting inner boundaries, the photon sphere, and the Innermost Stable Circular Orbit (ISCO) cleanly on a secondary display matrix.Extreme Physics Toggles: Allows the user to toggle relativistic phenomena (Doppler Beaming, Gravitational Redshift) independently to isolate and observe their singular impacts on the cosmic aesthetic.

Put this in the terminal to run the simulation:
python sim.py --quality high --spin 0.999

IM 14 BTW

title: Manas - Universal Sim Engine
author: Melvin Chirag
tagline: The universe is you, the universe you can envision...
description:
MANAS is a prototype for an interactive astrophysics simulation platform. I wanted to make something where the simulation is actually the main thing, not just a space visual with lots of effects around it.

Right now it has three modules: A Binary sytstem, a Pulsar Lab and a Black Hole Lab. In the Pulsar Lab, the pulse signal is based on the actual alignment between rotating beams and an observer, so changing the period or viewing geometry changes what gets detected. The Black Hole Lab is more of a visual environment right now, with parameters like mass and spin, plus a calculated Schwarzschild-radius scale.

The terminal only supports things that are already built into the project. The goal is eventually to have a place where you can load systems, change meaningful parameters, inspect what is happening, and see it play out in 3D.

This is still an early prototype, but I wanted to get the foundation right: interactive canvas, actual state behind the visuals, telemetry that is connected to the simulation, and being honest about what is calculated versus what is there to make the scene readable

title: The Void Project
author: Srimahi.D.R
tagline: Dance on the edge of the event horizon.
description:
This cinematic visual of a blackhole, Inspired by Gargantua in Interstellar, was made using real physics and Python + Taichi



Driven by authentic general relativity, the engine numerically integrates null geodesics through Kerr spacetime, mathematically capturing exactly how a spinning singularity warps the fabric of the universe. Every pixel you see is a ray of light bent by extreme gravitational lensing. 

The simulation features a physically grounded accretion disk modeled on the Novikov-Thorne temperature profile. It vividly demonstrates relativistic Doppler beaming—where light moving toward you appears blindingly intense, while receding light dims into the shadows. As you explore, you'll witness the iconic photon ring, the eerie silhouette of the event horizon, orbiting stellar debris, and high-energy relativistic jets blasting from the poles. 

 It’s not just a render, it’s an interactive laboratory for extreme physics. Made with amazing visuals and dancing jets,and stars in the background,so i believe its cool.

title: Gravitational waves
author: Parth S
tagline: Visualizing the birth of gravitational waves through binary black hole mergers.
description:
This simulation demonstrates the inspiral, merger, and ringdown of a binary black hole system and the resulting generation of gravitational waves. Two black holes with unequal masses orbit their common centre of mass while gradually losing orbital energy through gravitational-wave emission, causing their separation to decrease and their orbital frequency to increase. The simulation reproduces the characteristic gravitational-wave "chirp," where both the amplitude and frequency of the waveform increase until the moment of merger, followed by the damped ringdown of the final rotating (Kerr) black hole.

To enhance visual understanding, the simulation also depicts a hypothetical gas-rich environment surrounding the black holes. Three-dimensional plasma flows, photon-ring-inspired lensing, tidal streams, and merger ejecta are included as educational visualizations to illustrate how surrounding matter could be disturbed during a merger. These effects are not intended to represent every observed black hole merger, as many gravitational-wave events occur with little or no visible gas.

The project combines real-time 3D rendering with qualitative General Relativity concepts to create an interactive visualization that balances scientific accuracy with cinematic presentation, making complex astrophysical phenomena easier to understand.

title: Talk to this black hole or it'll die
author: Bhumik Khatwani
tagline: Well, start screaming to it or it'll literally evaporate.
description:
Say Hello to a Black Hole

I built a black hole that you have to talk to or it dies.

It's a real-time simulation of a Kerr-Newman black hole (spin, charge, the whole aggressive personality), ray-marched through actual curved spacetime on the GPU.

Speak into the mic and the accretion disk lights up as mass pours in, because apparently the way to a black hole's heart is just noise. 

Go quiet, and Hawking radiation kicks in, the hole slowly evaporating until it's basically ghosting you. 

A helical relativistic jet spirals out along the spin axis, and the whole thing sits against a Milky Way backdrop that actually bends around the photon sphere. Every number on screen, from the Schwarzschild radius to the evaporation timescale, is computed live. 

It's needy, it's physically accurate, and it will evaporate if you stop paying attention to it. 

Honestly, mood.

title: Schwarzschild Black Hole Ray Tracer
author: Tejasi G
tagline: A GPU-real-time render where every pixel is a photon traced backwards along an exact null geodesic of the Schwarzschild metric
description:
This is a real-time Schwarzschild black hole renderer where the black hole isn't drawn — it's *computed*. Every pixel on screen represents a photon fired backwards from a fixed cinematic camera (35 Schwarzschild radii out, 55° above the disk), and each one is traced along an exact null geodesic of the Schwarzschild metric using a fourth-order Runge–Kutta integrator. There's no lens-distortion shader faking the bending of light; the ray genuinely gets bent by solving the same equations that govern photon paths around a real non-rotating black hole, in geometric units where `G = c = M = 1`.

As each ray is integrated step by step, the simulation checks whether it crosses the equatorial plane between the photon sphere (3M) and 16M — if so, it's hit the accretion disk. There, it picks up a Shakura–Sunyaev-style intensity profile, gets Doppler-beamed and gravitationally redshifted based on the disk's orbital velocity and the photon's trajectory, and is converted through a blackbody-to-RGB mapping so the inner disk glows blue-white while the outer edge cools to red. Rays that don't hit the disk either fall past the event horizon and vanish, or escape to sample a procedurally generated starfield — gravitationally lensed along the way, which is what produces the warped background and the thin, glowing photon ring.

What makes it compelling is that nearly every visual feature — the shadow's sharp edge, the photon ring, the disk's asymmetric brightness, the lensed stars — falls directly out of solving real physics equations rather than being art-directed. It runs on GPU via Taichi, updates the disk's rotation live, and lets you speed up, slow down, or freeze time to watch how the geometry holds together frame by frame.
